VcPass_explanation: The target 'had heard' is a simple past perfect form (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'heard'), not the passive construction 'had been heard' required by the first CRI condition, and the subject 'Pharisees' is the semantic agent (the ones doing the hearing), not the patient.

FullText: Jesus answered : » If you know the gift of God and who asked you for a drink , you would have asked him to give you living water . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'would have asked' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'you' and an overt direct object 'him', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'asked' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you'), making it transitive and not unaccusative; it does not meet any of the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'asked' licenses an internal argument ('him'), which is the indirect object receiving the request, so it does not meet the criterion of having no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'would have asked him to give you living water' involves a directed action toward a defined goal (obtaining living water), satisfying the fourth CRI condition of directed action toward an abstract goal that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' denotes a volitional speech act with internal duration and process structure, not a punctual transition into a new state; it cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial without awkwardness, and it permits interruption (one can meaningfully 'stop asking').
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' is a punctual, telic action denoting a single discrete speech act with a defined endpoint (making a request), not a dynamic durative activity that can be modified by 'for X time' or decomposed into homogeneous subintervals.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' construes a dynamic event that entails a resulting change of state (the addressee being requested to perform an action), not a punctual occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'would have asked him (to give)' denotes a dynamic action requiring volitional engagement and internal eventive unfolding (asking involves intentional communicative activity), not a static state or condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If you know...', expressing an unfulfilled past condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have asked him (to give)' contains no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), no negative quantifier, no 'never' adverb, and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would have asked him (to give)' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical action, not an experiential perfect claiming occurrence or absence of an event in the subject's history, and contains no explicit frequency adverbs or perception/communication verbs focused on experience occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have asked') expressing what did not actually happen, not a description of a current resulting state; none of the CRI conditions apply because the action is hypothetical and the resulting state is not currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical past action, not a situation extending to reference time, non-terminating predicate, habitual series, or continuous situation from a starting point.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause presents a counterfactual conditional with 'would have asked' (past habitual/modal + perfect), which construes the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint—a discrete hypothetical action that would have been completed as a whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have asked' in a conditional construction ('If you know...you would have asked'), presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical situation rather than a realized one.
TLTS_explanation: The gram includes a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the third set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have asked him (to give)' is active voice (subject 'you' is the agent performing the asking), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'have been + past participle' structure and does not have the grammatical subject as semantic patient.

FullText: » For you have had five husbands . The one you have now is not your husband , this is true . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have had' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('five husbands'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') and denotes possession, not a change-of-state, motion, existence, or copular relation, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have had' licenses and realizes an internal argument ('five husbands'), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have had (five husbands)' denotes a stative possession of a completed experience without a durative process toward a goal, endpoint modification by 'in X time', gradual change-of-state culmination, or directed motion toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have had' denotes a state of possession with extended duration rather than a punctual transition; it cannot be modified by a specific time-point adverbial (*'have had at noon'), permits interruption conceptually, and has measurable internal duration spanning the time period of the five marriages.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have had' denotes a completed, punctual state of possession (five husbands total), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have had five husbands' is construed as a resultative state (a change of marital status with lasting consequences), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have had' denotes possession of a stable relational configuration (having husbands as a state of past experience), which is a stative condition that does not require active effort or internal dynamic development to maintain.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have had' is a simple past perfect statement of fact ('you have had five husbands'), not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure; the discourse presents this as an actual occurrence, not contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have had' is not negated with 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject/object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'have had (five husbands)' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have had (five husbands)' is a Perfect construction denoting a bounded activity (marrying/having husbands) where the focus is on having undergone the experience of acquiring five husbands across the subject's history, not on a persisting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have had' describes a change of possession/marital status (marriage), and the context indicates this status is currently held at the reference time, as evidenced by the immediate follow-up 'The one you have now is not your husband.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains 'have had five husbands' which denotes a repeated series of events (five separate marriages) that characterizes the entire time span from past to present reference time, satisfying the habitual/repeated events criterion.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause presents 'have had five husbands' as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint (a completed series of five marriages), rather than as ongoing or habitual, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have had' is a present perfect expressing a realized past state (completed action with present relevance), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have had' contains 'had', which is the morphologically past form of the auxiliary 'have', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have had' is active voice (present perfect of 'have'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the subject 'you' is the agent, not the patient.

FullText: » Come see a man who told me everything I have ever done . Could this be the Christ ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have done' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'I' and an overt direct object 'everything', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have done' is a transitive construction that licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'I') and an internal argument (the object 'everything'), so it does not meet any of the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'everything'), so it does not meet any of the four conditions for ASUnrg, which all require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' in this context refers to a completed action without a specific bounded endpoint or durative process directed toward a goal; it describes a state of completion rather than an aktionsart with telic properties.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' denotes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it permits internal duration (actions can be interrupted mid-performance), does not naturally modify with specific time-point adverbials in this context, and lacks the instantaneous boundary quality required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' denotes a completed action with relevance to the present, not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and it is not homogeneous or iterative in the way activities are.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' refers to a completed action with resulting change of state (accumulated life experiences), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done' denotes completed actions with internal dynamic development and eventive unfolding, not a static state or condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' occurs in a relative clause describing past actions in a factual context, not in a counterfactual conditional, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, contrary-to-fact discourse, or inverted conditional protasis.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done' is modified by 'ever' (not 'never'), has no negated auxiliary, contains no negative quantifiers, and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have done' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions; 'ever' is an aspectual adverb, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the explicit frequency adverb 'ever' ('have ever done'), which indicates a claim about the subject's experiential history across distinct instances.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' is a present perfect used to describe past actions within a lifetime span, not to indicate a resulting state currently in effect; none of the CRI conditions for DtRes (change of state, possession, status, or motion with resulting state) apply.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have ever done' with the universal quantifier 'ever' denotes a situation (all actions performed) that extends from the past to the reference time, satisfying the criterion of a non-terminating situation holding throughout the entire time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have ever done' presents a habitual or iterative situation (everything across a lifetime) rather than a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, and none of the other CRI conditions apply.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done' is a present perfect expressing a realized state (actions completed up to the present moment), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have done' is present perfect (not past auxiliary, future marking, modal + have, or been + progressive), so none of the set_to_1_if conditions apply.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have done' is present perfect active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the subject is not a semantic patient.

FullText: Many Samaritans of that city put faith believed in him because of what the woman said : » He told me everything I have ever done . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have done' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'I' and an overt direct object 'everything', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'I'), making it accusative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have done' licenses an internal argument (the implied direct object 'everything'), as evidenced by the context 'everything I have ever done,' so it does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions which require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' in this context refers to a completed action without a defined endpoint or goal-directed process; it expresses a life experience ('everything I have ever done') rather than a durative process toward a bounded result, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion duration.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' denotes a completed action with relevance to the present moment but lacks the punctual, instantaneous transition into a new state characteristic of achievement verbs, does not naturally modify with specific time-point adverbials in this context, permits internal duration and process phases, and does not represent a perceptual or cognitive transition into awareness.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' denotes a completed action with relevance to the present moment, not an ongoing dynamic activity; it fails all four CRI conditions as it is punctual/telic rather than durative/atelic.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' refers to a completed action with resulting change of state (the woman's past deeds/life events), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done' denotes completed actions with internal dynamic development and eventive unfolding, not a static state, mental condition, or stable relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' occurs in a direct speech report ('He told me everything I have ever done') describing past actions that actually occurred, not in a counterfactual conditional, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done' is modified by 'ever' (not 'never'), has no negated auxiliary, no negative quantifier, and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have ever done' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions; 'ever' is an aspectual/frequency adverb, not a past time adverb.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have ever done' contains the explicit frequency adverb 'ever', which indicates a claim about distinct instances across the subject's history, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' is a present perfect used to describe the woman's life experiences up to the present moment, not a resulting state from a change of state, possession, status, or motion; it expresses a temporal relationship rather than a current resultant state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have ever done' contains the duration adverbial 'ever' indicating a situation extending across the entire lifetime to the reference time, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The phrase 'have ever done' expresses a habitual or iterative sense (everything across a lifetime) rather than a single bounded occurrence, and does not match any of the set_to_1_if conditions for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done' is a present perfect expressing a completed action within a time period extending to the present, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, subjunctive, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have done' is present perfect (not past auxiliary, future marking, modal + have, or been + progressive), so none of the set_to_1_if conditions apply.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have done' is present perfect active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle, and lacks the structural and semantic features required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus saw him lying there . He knew that he had been sick for a long time . So he asked him : » Do you want to get well ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'had been sick' is a passive construction with no overt external agent, no direct object, and does not license two internal arguments; it only has an implicit subject 'he' and the adjective 'sick' as a subject complement, not an object.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been sick' is a copular clause with 'be' where the sole overt argument 'he' functions as the syntactic subject linked to the predicative complement 'sick', and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been sick' is a stative predicate with an overt subject 'he' but does not denote an activity or manner of motion; it expresses a state, and none of the four CRI conditions for ASUnrg (which require activity verbs with external arguments and no internal arguments) apply.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been sick' describes a stative condition without a defined endpoint, goal, or result state; it cannot be modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion, and does not denote directed change toward a culminating result.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been sick' denotes a state with measurable internal duration ('for a long time'), not a punctual transition, and cannot be modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievement verbs can, failing all AktAch criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had been sick' denotes a stative condition rather than a dynamic action; it describes a state of being rather than an ongoing activity, and sickness is not homogeneous in the way activities are (subintervals of being sick are not themselves instances of the action of 'being sick').
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been sick' describes a state of being ill over an extended duration ('for a long time'), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had been sick' denotes a static physical condition that holds over time without internal dynamic development or active volitional engagement, satisfying multiple CRI conditions for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had been sick' occurs in a narrative past perfect construction describing a factual state of affairs (the man's actual illness), not in a counterfactual conditional, inverted conditional, or unfulfilled wish context.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been sick' contains no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), no negative quantifier, no 'never' adverb, and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he had been sick for a long time' contains no lexical past time adverb, no phrasal past time adverb, and no clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions; 'for a long time' is a duration phrase, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had been sick' is a negative Perfect (past perfect of a state) that functions as a claim about the subject's experiential history, indicating the occurrence of sickness as a bounded condition in the past.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been sick' describes a past state that is no longer in effect at the reference time (Jesus asks if he wants to get well, implying he is currently sick but the clause uses past perfect to indicate a prior duration, not a resulting state currently in effect).
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time' indicating a situation that extends to the reference time, and the predicate 'had been sick' denotes a non-terminating state holding throughout that entire span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been sick for a long time' presents the situation as ongoing and durative rather than bounded; the adverbial 'for a long time' profiles duration, not completion, and the predicate 'sick' is a state, not a transition event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been sick' is a past perfect form expressing a realized state in the past, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been sick' contains the auxiliary 'had' which is morphologically past, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been sick' lacks a past participle of a transitive verb (sick is an adjective, not a participle), lacks a semantic patient as subject, and lacks a by-phrase; none of the VcPass criteria are met.

FullText: » The Father does not judge any man for he has given all judgment to the Son .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has given' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt direct object ('all judgment'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent) and an internal argument (all judgment as theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet any unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' introduces an overt subject ('he') and licenses an internal argument ('all judgment'), so it does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions which require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has given all judgment' involves a durative process of transferring judgment directed toward a defined goal (the Son receiving judgment), which is a bounded accomplishment that can be modified by 'in X time' and denotes completion of a transfer action.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of judgment from Father to Son) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple AktAch criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed, punctual transfer of judgment (a perfective accomplishment), not a dynamic, durative, homogeneous activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given all judgment' is construed as a completed action that entails a resulting change of state (the Son now possesses judgment authority), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer action with internal dynamic development and volitional engagement, not a stative condition, mental state, or stable relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has given' occurs in a causal clause ('for he has given all judgment to the Son') that states a factual reason, not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has given' is not negated with 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in its immediate clause, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the negation in the sentence ('does not judge') applies to a different verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has given all judgment' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The Perfect 'has given' expresses a completed transfer of judgment resulting in a current state (the Son now possesses all judgment), not an experience or repeated instance, so none of the DtExp criteria apply.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a change of possession (transfer of judgment authority), and the context indicates this status is currently held by the Son ('all judgment to the Son').
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has given all judgment to the Son' uses present perfect to denote a completed transfer of authority, not a situation extending to reference time, a non-terminating state, habitual repetition, or continuous holding from a starting point.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' presents a completed transfer of judgment as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' is present perfect indicating a completed action in past time with present relevance, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, subjunctive, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given' is present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'given'), which does not match any of the four set_to_1_if conditions: it is not morphologically past, lacks future marking, contains no modal auxiliary before 'have', and does not include 'been' followed by a progressive participle.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'give'), not passive voice; the subject 'he' (the Father) is the agent performing the action, not the patient; and there is no 'by'-phrase expressing an external argument.

FullText: » Truly , Truly I say to you , he who hears my word , and believes him that sent me , has everlasting life . He will not receive judgment for he has passed out of death into life .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has passed out' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'of death' (prepositional phrase functioning as object), satisfying the first CRI condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'passed out' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he'), making it accusative rather than unaccusative; the subject is an agent undergoing directed motion/transition, not a pure theme/patient with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has passed out' licenses an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'of death' functioning as a source/origin complement), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event denotes a gradual change-of-state that culminates in a defined result condition: transitioning from death into life, which is a bounded achievement with a clear endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has passed out of death' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from death to life), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials, does not permit interruption, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple AktAch criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has passed out' denotes a punctual, telic transition from one state to another (death to life), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' describes a transition between states (from death to life) that entails a resulting change of state, not a punctual dynamic occurrence without state change.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has passed out of death' denotes a dynamic transition or change of state (movement from death to life), which involves eventive unfolding and does not represent a static mental state, emotion, or stable relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has passed out of death' occurs in a statement of fact about a present state ('has everlasting life'), not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact context.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has passed out' is not negated with 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject/object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has passed out of death into life' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has passed out' denotes a bounded activity/transition (moving from death into life) where the focus is on having undergone the experience of this transition, not on a persisting result, satisfying the fourth CRI condition for DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'passed out of death' describes a change of state (from death to life), and the context 'into life' indicates the resulting state (being alive/in life) is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has passed out of death' denotes a completed transition event (perfective aspect) rather than a duration extending to reference time, lacks duration adverbials, does not describe a non-terminating situation, and contains no habitual/repeated characterization or 'since' construction.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'passed out of death' is a transition event (moving from death to life) construed as a completed whole, satisfying the third CRI condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has passed out of death' is in present perfect tense expressing a realized state, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future construction, and does not occur in a subjunctive or optative context.
TLTS_explanation: The target gram 'has passed out' contains the auxiliary 'has' in present perfect form, which instantiates TLTS (Tense-Locus-Time-Span) as a past participle construction indicating a completed action with present relevance.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has passed out of death' does not instantiate VcPass because it lacks the required auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle structure; 'passed' is a simple past participle without 'been', and the subject 'he' is the agent performing the action, not the patient being affected.

FullText: » You sent people to John the Baptist and he has witnessed about the truth .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'about the truth', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') that performs the action of witnessing, and the sole argument does not function as an internal theme/patient undergoing change-of-state or motion, so it does not meet any unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' licenses an internal argument ('about the truth'), which is a prepositional object, so it does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions requiring absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' denotes a state of having perceived or observed something, which is neither a durative process toward a defined goal, nor naturally modified by 'in X time', nor a gradual change-of-state, nor directed motion toward a goal—it lacks the telic, bounded, result-oriented character required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (seeing/perceiving truth), satisfying CRI condition 4, and can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'has witnessed at that moment'), satisfying CRI condition 2.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' denotes a punctual, telic event of perceiving/observing testimony about truth, which cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, fails the homogeneity test, and does not represent an ongoing durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has witnessed' is construed as a durative state of knowledge/perception resulting from a past action, not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' denotes a dynamic, eventive action of observing and testifying about something, which involves internal development and volitional engagement, failing to meet any of the stative conditions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' occurs in a simple declarative statement about a past event that actually occurred, not in a counterfactual conditional, inverted conditional, or expression of unfulfilled wish, and the discourse contains no markers indicating the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has witnessed' is not negated with 'not'/'n't', is not modified by 'never', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has witnessed about the truth' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has witnessed' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of an experience (having witnessed about the truth) rather than on a resulting state, matching the third CRI condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'witnessed' describes a past action of bearing testimony, not a change of state, possession, status, or motion to a destination; the context does not indicate a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has witnessed' denotes a non-terminating situation (witnessing about the truth) that extends from a past point to the reference time, satisfying the criterion that the predicate denotes a situation holding throughout the entire time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has witnessed' presents a completed act of testifying as a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, rather than as an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' is a present perfect form expressing a realized, completed action in the past, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' is present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'witnessed'), which does not match any of the four set_to_1_if conditions: it lacks past morphology on the auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary before 'have', or 'been' followed by a progressive participle.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has witnessed' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'witness'), not passive voice; the subject 'he' is the agent performing the witnessing, not the patient; and there is no 'by'-phrase.

FullText: » The testimony I have is greater than that of John . The works I do are the works the Father gave me to accomplish . They testify that the Father has sent me .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an external argument (the Father as agent) and an internal argument (me as theme), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument ('me'), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action of directed motion/transfer toward a defined goal (the speaker being sent by the Father), which is a bounded event with a clear endpoint and result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending/dispatching) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple AktAch criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual, telic event (a completed action of sending) that cannot be naturally modified by durative 'for X time' adverbials and is not homogeneous across subintervals, failing all criteria for AktAct classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (the Father causing the subject to be in a sent/dispatched condition), which disqualifies it from the punctual dynamic occurrence feature that excludes resulting state changes.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed dynamic action (sending) that involves volitional engagement and eventive unfolding, not a static state, mental condition, or stable relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has sent' occurs in a declarative statement about a past action that is asserted as fact, not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The Perfect 'has sent' expresses a resulting state (the Father's act of sending has resulted in the speaker's current presence/mission) rather than focusing on the occurrence of the experience itself or bounded activity, and no explicit frequency adverbs or negative marking are present.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a change of possession/status (the Father sending the speaker as an agent), and the context indicates this resulting state (being sent/commissioned) is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' uses present perfect to denote a completed past action with present relevance, not a situation extending to reference time, non-terminating throughout a span, habitual series, or continuous holding since a point.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (sending as a completed action with a construed endpoint) presented as a bounded occurrence in the narrative context of testimony about the Father's action.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have not heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'You' and an overt direct object 'his voice', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You'), making it transitive and not unaccusative; it does not meet any of the CRI conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his voice'), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have not heard' describes a stative condition of non-experience rather than a durative process directed toward a goal, cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate completion, does not denote gradual change-of-state culminating in a result, and does not involve directed motion or action toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing/perceiving sound), which matches the CRI criterion for punctual perceptual or cognitive transitions into recognition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have not heard' denotes a stative, non-dynamic situation (lack of perceptual experience) that cannot be naturally modified by durative 'for X time' adverbials and is not homogeneous or iterative, failing all CRI conditions for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have not heard' is construed as a stative/experiential condition (lack of perceptual experience) rather than a single punctual dynamic occurrence, and it entails a resulting state of non-acquaintance, not a momentary action like 'knock' or 'tap'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have not heard' denotes a dynamic perceptual event (the act of hearing/listening) that requires volitional engagement and represents a completed action rather than a static state or condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' is a simple present perfect statement of fact about the addressees' actual experience, not a counterfactual conditional, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'have' of the target gram 'have not heard' is negated with 'not', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'at any time' is a negative polarity expression, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' is a negative Perfect that functions as a claim about the absence of the event (hearing the Father's voice) in the subject's history, matching the second set_to_1_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'heard' describes a change of state (acquiring perceptual knowledge), and the negation 'have not heard' indicates the undergoer remains in the state of not having heard his voice, which is currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice' denotes a negative existential state (absence of an experience) rather than a non-terminating situation extending to reference time, lacks duration adverbials, and contains no 'since' marker indicating continuous duration from a past point.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'You have not heard his voice' presents a negated state of non-occurrence across an extended time period ('at any time'), not a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, and does not match any other set_to_1_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' is a present perfect form expressing a realized state (lack of past experience up to now), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' is present perfect (not past perfect, future perfect, modal perfect, or progressive perfect), so none of the set_to_1_if conditions apply.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have not heard' is active voice (subject 'You' is the agent performing the hearing action), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and shows no semantic patient as grammatical subject.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject (understood 'you' from context) and an overt direct object ('him'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you') and an internal argument ('him'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet any unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('him'), so it does not meet any of the four conditions for ASUnrg, which all require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'seen' in this context denotes a stative perception event with no defined endpoint, result state, or directed goal—it describes a state of not having experienced visual perception, which does not satisfy any of the four AktAcc criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (perceiving/recognizing someone visually), and the phrase 'at any time' functions as a specific temporal boundary adverbial modifying the event, satisfying two CRI conditions.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'seen' is a perfective past participle denoting a punctual, completed perceptual event that cannot be naturally modified by durative 'for X time' adverbials (*'seen him for an hour' is ungrammatical), and does not exhibit the homogeneity property required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'seen' in this context entails a resulting change of state (having perceived/witnessed someone), not a single punctual occurrence without consequences like 'knock' or 'blink'.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'seen' denotes a dynamic perceptual event (witnessing/observing someone) rather than a stative mental state; it describes a completed action with internal temporal development, not a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' occurs in a simple past negation statement ('You have not...nor seen him at any time') expressing a factual claim about what has not occurred, not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' appears in a 'neither…nor' construction ('nor seen him') that expresses negation, satisfying the fourth CRI condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'nor seen him at any time' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'at any time' is a general temporal expression, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The gram is a negative Perfect ('have not seen'), which functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's history, matching the second set_to_1_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'seen' describes a change of state (perception/experience) where the undergoer (him) has not been perceived, and this negative state of non-perception is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'nor seen him at any time' contains a perfective aspect with a negative polarity and temporal boundary marker 'at any time', which indicates a bounded situation rather than a durative situation extending to reference time, and lacks duration adverbials, non-terminating predicates, habitual markers, or 'since' constructions required by the CRIs.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'seen' is a transition event (perceiving/noticing) construed as a completed whole in a bounded negative assertion ('nor seen him at any time'), presenting a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is in present perfect tense expressing a realized state (not having seen him), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' lacks morphological past marking on the auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary preceding 'have', or 'been' followed by a progressive participle, so none of the set_to_1_if conditions are met.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have seen' lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure (it is 'have seen' not 'have been seen'), and there is no 'by'-phrase; additionally, 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of seeing, not the patient.

FullText: » You do not have his word dwelling in you . This is because you do not believe in the person he has sent .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sent' licenses an external argument (the person 'he' who performs the sending action), making it accusative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument ('the person'), so it does not meet any of the four conditions for ASUnrg, which all require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (a person having been sent), which is a bounded event with a clear endpoint, satisfying the criterion of a durative process directed toward a defined goal or result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending is completed at a specific moment), can be modified by time-point adverbials ('he has sent at that moment'), does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, and lacks measurable internal duration, all satisfying the AktAch feature criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual, telic action with a defined endpoint (the act of sending is complete), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (the person is now in a sent state/position), which disqualifies it from the AktSemel feature that requires no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed dynamic action with internal development (the act of sending), requiring volitional engagement and active effort, thus failing all four AktState criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'has sent' occurs in a relative clause describing a factual person (the one he has sent), not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' is not negated with 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the negation in the clause ('do not believe') applies to the verb 'believe', not to 'has sent'.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has sent' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'the person he has sent' uses a Perfect form to establish a completed action with present relevance (identifying who the person is), not to express a distinct temporal experience, frequency, absence, or bounded activity focus as required by the CRIs.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a completed action of sending, but the text does not indicate that a resulting state (such as the person being in a sent condition or remaining at a destination) is currently in effect; rather, it simply establishes that the sending action occurred in the past.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'the person he has sent' uses present perfect to reference a completed past action without duration adverbials, non-terminating predicates, habitual series, or 'since', so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (sending as a completed action) construed as a bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, satisfying the third CRI condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is a present perfect indicative form expressing a completed action in past time with present relevance, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, subjunctive, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'sent'), which does not match any of the four conditions: it is not morphologically past, contains no future marking, has no modal auxiliary before 'have', and does not include 'been' followed by a progressive participle.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has sent' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'sent'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' component and the subject 'he' is the agent, not the patient.

FullText: » Do not think that I will accuse you before the Father . Moses on whom you have set your hope is a plaintiff against you .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'set' in 'have set your hope' is transitive and licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you'), with 'hope' as the internal argument (theme), so it does not meet any unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an internal argument ('your hope' as direct object), so it does not meet any of the four conditions for ASUnrg, which all require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' describes a completed mental state rather than a durative process directed toward a goal, does not naturally accept 'in X time' modification, does not denote gradual change-of-state culminating in a result, and does not involve directed motion toward a spatial or abstract goal.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a durative state of maintaining hope rather than a punctual transition; it does not satisfy any CRI condition as it permits interruption, lacks a natural time-point boundary reading, and involves sustained internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a punctual, perfective action of establishing hope (a completed mental act), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' describes a resulting change of state (establishing hope in someone), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' denotes a volitional action of placing or directing hope toward someone, which involves active effort and eventive unfolding, failing to meet any of the stative conditions.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect indicative statement of fact about the addressees' actual hope in Moses, not a counterfactual condition, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'have set your hope' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The Perfect gram 'have set your hope' denotes a bounded activity (the act of placing hope) where the focus is on having undergone the experience of setting hope, not on a persisting resulting state, which matches the fourth CRI condition for DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set' describes a change of state (placing hope in someone), and the context indicates this state of having hope placed on Moses is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'you have set your hope' uses present perfect aspect describing a completed action with present relevance, not a non-terminating situation extending to reference time, lacks duration adverbials, lacks habitual/repeated characterization, and lacks 'since'; none of the set_to_1_if conditions apply.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have set your hope' presents a state resulting from a past action rather than a single bounded occurrence; it describes an ongoing mental state of reliance, not a transition event or discrete narrative step, and lacks completion-measure adverbials or passive 'have been' + participle structure.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect indicative construction expressing a realized state (completed action with present relevance), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, subjunctive, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect construction with 'have' as the auxiliary and 'set' as the past participle, but none of the four CRI conditions apply: the auxiliary is not morphologically past, there is no future marking, no modal precedes 'have', and there is no 'been' followed by a progressive participle.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have set your hope' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'set' of a transitive verb with 'you' as agent), not passive voice with the required form 'have been set', and lacks a 'by'-phrase or patient subject.

FullText: They gathered twelve baskets of leftovers after everyone had eaten .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had eaten' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'everyone' and licenses an internal argument (the understood direct object of eating), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive verb with subject and object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'eat' licenses an external argument (the agent 'everyone' who performs the eating action), so it does not meet any of the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' licenses an internal argument (the understood direct object 'food/leftovers'), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had eaten' denotes a completed action without a defined goal, endpoint, or result state; it is not naturally modified by 'in X time' to indicate bounded completion, does not involve gradual change-of-state culmination, and does not involve directed motion toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a durative process with measurable internal duration (the act of consuming food), not a punctual transition; it permits interruption (one can stop eating mid-meal), and does not naturally occur at a defined temporal boundary like 'at noon'.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a completed, punctual event with a clear endpoint (finishing a meal), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had eaten' entails a resulting change of state (consumption of food), so it does not match the criterion for a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a completed dynamic action involving internal change (consumption of food), not a mental state, possession, static perception, or stative condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had eaten' is a past perfect in a temporal clause ('after everyone had eaten') describing a sequence of actual events, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or contrary-to-fact situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'after', a temporal conjunction that introduces a past time reference frame for the target verb 'had eaten'.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'after everyone had eaten' uses past perfect to establish temporal sequence and a resulting state (leftovers remained), not to express distinct experiential instances or bounded activity focus.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' describes a completed action with no resulting state currently in effect; the focus is on the prior completion of eating, not on a present state of being eaten or a change of state that persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had eaten' is a past perfect denoting a completed action prior to gathering leftovers, not a situation extending to reference time, non-terminating throughout a span, habitual/repeated characterizing events, or continuous holding from a starting point—none of the set_to_1_if conditions apply.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'everyone had eaten' presents eating as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint (completion of the meal), satisfying the first CRI condition for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had eaten' is a past perfect form describing a completed action in the past, not a modal auxiliary, conditional/counterfactual construction, subjunctive context, or future projection.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had eaten' lacks the required 'been' component (should be 'had been eaten'), the subject 'everyone' is the agent not the patient, and there is no 'by'-phrase, so none of the VcPass conditions are met.

FullText: They boarded a boat to travel over the sea to Capernaum . It was dark and Jesus had not yet come to them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' has an overt subject 'Jesus' but no overt direct object, and the infinitival complement 'to them' is a prepositional phrase (indirect), not a direct object, so none of the three conditions for ASTrns are met.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument 'Jesus' undergoes displacement to the location 'to them', with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument ('to them'), so it does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions which require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'come to them' involves directed motion toward a spatial goal (reaching the disciples) that defines completion, matching the fourth CRI condition.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/appearance), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple AktAch criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'come' is punctual and telic (denotes arrival at a goal state), not dynamic and durative; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'come' construed as 'had not yet come' entails a resulting change of state (Jesus's arrival/presence), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had not yet come' denotes a dynamic event of arrival/movement with internal temporal development, not a static state, mental condition, or stable relational configuration.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had not yet come' is a simple past perfect statement describing a factual situation (Jesus had not arrived), not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' of the target gram 'had not yet come' is negated with 'not', satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor; 'yet' is an aspectual adverb, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The gram is a negative Perfect ('had not yet come') which functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's history, matching the second set_to_1_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'come' is a motion verb, but the context indicates Jesus had NOT yet arrived at the destination (negation 'had not yet come'), so the resulting state of arrival is not currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had not yet come' is a perfective past construction indicating a completed non-occurrence at a reference point; it does not instantiate duration adverbials, non-terminating situations, habitual series, or 'since' constructions required by the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' presents a negative state of non-arrival rather than a bounded completed occurrence; it describes what has not happened up to a reference point, not a transition event construed as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not yet come' is a past perfect construction describing a realized state in the past narrative (Jesus had not arrived by that point in time), not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, subjunctive, or future-marked construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet come' is a simple past perfect form of an intransitive verb ('come'), not an auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle construction required for passive voice, and contains no agent or patient relationship.

FullText: » Do not work for food that perishes but for food that offers everlasting life . The Son of man will give you this food . The Father , even God , has set his seal upon him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has set his seal upon' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The Father, even God') and an overt direct object ('his seal'), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an external argument (The Father/God as agent) that performs the action of setting the seal, making it accusative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has set' licenses an internal argument ('his seal') and a prepositional phrase ('upon him'), so it does not meet the criterion of introducing a subject without licensing internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has set his seal upon him' involves a directed action toward a defined goal (marking/authenticating the Son of man with a seal), culminating in a result state where the seal is placed, satisfying the criteria for a bounded, goal-directed event.

AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'has set his seal upon' denotes a punctual, telic action with a defined endpoint (the completion of sealing), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying completion, and subintervals do not instantiate the same event type.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has set his seal upon' describes a resulting change of state (marking/authenticating someone), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without resulting change like 'knock' or 'tap'.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has set his seal upon' denotes a completed action of placing/marking with a seal, which is a dynamic, volitional, eventive act requiring active effort, not a state that holds over time.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has set his seal upon' contains no negated auxiliary, negative quantifier, 'never' adverb, or 'neither…nor' construction.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has set his seal upon him' contains no lexical past time adverb, phrasal past time adverb, or clausal past time anchor with temporal conjunctions.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is a Perfect expressing a resulting state (God's seal is currently upon him) rather than instantiating any of the four DtExp conditions: it lacks explicit frequency adverbs, is not negative, does not focus on perception/communication experience, and emphasizes the persisting result rather than the bounded activity itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set his seal upon' describes a change of status (marking/authorization), and the context indicates this resulting state is currently in effect, as evidenced by the present perfect tense and the clause structure showing the seal remains upon him.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has set his seal upon' is a perfective action (completed past event) with no duration adverbials, non-terminating situation, habitual series, or 'since' clause indicating extension to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'set his seal upon' is a transition event (a completed action of marking/authenticating) construed as a bounded whole, and the clause presents this as a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is present perfect indicative expressing a realized past action, not a modal, conditional, counterfactual, hypothetical, or future construction.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'has' is morphologically present perfect, which combined with the past participle 'set' forms a present perfect construction that instantiates TLTS (Tense-Locus-Time-Span) marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has set his seal upon' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'set'), not passive voice; the subject 'The Father' is the agent performing the action, not the patient being affected.

FullText: Jesus answered : » This is the work of God , that you have an active faith in the one whom he has sent . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'the one' (relative pronoun), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' is transitive and licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he'), making it accusative rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'the one'), making it transitive and not matching any of the four conditions for ASUnrg, which all require absence of internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (someone has been sent), which is a bounded event that can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'he has sent in an hour'), satisfying the AktAcc feature criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending is completed at a specific moment), can be modified by time-point adverbials ('he has sent at dawn'), does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, and lacks measurable internal duration, all satisfying the AktAch feature criteria.
